skill.name: secure-linux-web-hosting
skill.description: Use when setting up, hardening, or reviewing a cloud server for self-hosting, including DNS, SSH, firewalls, Nginx, static-site hosting, reverse-proxying an app, HTTPS with Let's Encrypt or ACME clients, safe HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects, or optional post-launch network tuning such as BBR.
skill.summary: Transform a cloud server into a secure web host with structured guidance for DNS, SSH, firewalls, Nginx, HTTPS, and optional performance tuning.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analyzer flagged 92 patterns, but all are false positives. The skill contains markdown documentation only, not executable code. Backticks detected are markdown formatting for code examples, URLs are documentation references, and diagnostic commands (dig, ss, curl) are standard Linux tools for validation. The skill teaches secure hosting practices and poses no security risk.

content.user_title: Deploy and Secure Linux Web Servers
content.value_statement: Setting up a secure cloud server requires navigating distro differences and avoiding common pitfalls. This skill provides a structured, verified workflow for SSH hardening, firewall configuration, Nginx setup, HTTPS deployment, and optional performance tuning.
actual_capabilities: Guide through distro identification and routing for Debian, Ubuntu, Fedora, RHEL, Rocky, AlmaLinux, and Arch systems
Provide step-by-step SSH hardening with safety gates to prevent lockout
Configure firewall rules with deny-by-default posture using ufw, firewalld, or nftables
Set up Nginx for static site hosting or reverse proxy to localhost applications
Issue and manage TLS certificates using Certbot or acme.sh
Validate each phase before proceeding to prevent cascading failures
limitations: Does not deploy to Kubernetes, PaaS platforms, or container orchestration systems
Focused on single-server Linux hosting, not multi-server architecture design
Does not cover Windows or macOS host administration
Requires user to have root or admin access to the target server

use_cases.title: Deploy a Personal Static Website
use_cases.description: Set up a cloud server to host a static portfolio or blog site with HTTPS, starting from a fresh Linux installation with no prior configuration.
use_cases.target_user: Developer or hobbyist deploying a personal project

use_cases.title: Secure an Existing Server
use_cases.description: Review and harden SSH configuration, firewall rules, and web server security on a server that is already running but may have insecure defaults.
use_cases.target_user: System administrator or developer managing production infrastructure

use_cases.title: Host a Web Application Behind Nginx
use_cases.description: Configure Nginx as a reverse proxy for a Node.js, Python, or other application that listens on localhost, keeping the app private while exposing only Nginx publicly.
use_cases.target_user: Full-stack developer deploying a web application

prompt_templates.title: Fresh Server Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a fresh Ubuntu 22.04 server with root access. I want to host my static portfolio site at example.com with HTTPS. Walk me through the complete setup from SSH login to a working HTTPS site.

prompt_templates.title: SSH Hardening Review
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm logged into my Debian server and want to harden SSH access. I have a second SSH session open as a safety backup. What settings should I change and in what order?

prompt_templates.title: Reverse Proxy Configuration
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a Node.js app running on 127.0.0.1:3000 on my Fedora server. Set up Nginx to proxy requests from my domain to the app, then secure it with Let's Encrypt.

prompt_templates.title: Troubleshoot HTTPS Issues
prompt_templates.prompt: My Let's Encrypt certificate issuance keeps failing. DNS points to my server, HTTP works on port 80, but Certbot says validation failed. Help me diagnose the issue.

output_examples.input: User wants to host a static site on Ubuntu with HTTPS
output: Phase 1: Verify server access and install updates
Phase 2: Configure firewall (allow SSH, HTTP, HTTPS)
Phase 3: Install and configure Nginx with document root
Phase 4: Confirm HTTP site loads before certificate request
Phase 5: Issue Let's Encrypt certificate with Certbot
Phase 6: Enable HTTP-to-HTTPS redirect after verifying HTTPS works

output_examples.input: User locked out after SSH hardening from only session
output: Recovery steps: Access server via provider console or rescue mode
Restore SSH config from backup or reset to defaults
Test key-based login in a separate session before making changes
Re-apply hardening changes incrementally with validation between each

best_practices: Always test SSH changes in a second session before closing the first connection
Verify HTTP works before requesting certificates to avoid ACME validation failures
Keep application backends on localhost with Nginx as the only public-facing service
anti_patterns: Applying SSH hardening from the only active session without a recovery path
Enabling HTTP-to-HTTPS redirects before confirming HTTPS loads correctly
Using outdated distro-specific commands from old tutorials without verification

faq.question: What Linux distributions does this skill support?
faq.answer: This skill supports Debian, Ubuntu, Fedora, RHEL, Rocky Linux, AlmaLinux, and Arch. It verifies distro-specific commands against official documentation before providing actionable steps.

faq.question: Can this skill deploy my application code?
faq.answer: No. This skill focuses on server setup, security hardening, and web server configuration. It can configure Nginx to proxy to your app, but does not handle application deployment or CI/CD.

faq.question: What if I lose SSH access during hardening?
faq.answer: Always maintain a second SSH session when making SSH changes. If locked out, use your cloud provider's console access or rescue mode to restore access.

faq.question: Should I use Certbot or acme.sh for certificates?
faq.answer: Both are valid. Certbot offers distro-packaged installation and broad documentation. acme.sh is lighter weight. Choose based on your preference and verify the current instructions for your chosen client.

faq.question: Is BBR tuning required for good performance?
faq.answer: No. BBR and kernel tuning are optional post-setup optimizations. Complete the secure hosting flow first, then consider tuning only if you have specific performance needs.

faq.question: Can this skill help if my server is already configured?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill can review existing configurations, identify security gaps, and provide a prioritized remediation sequence without requiring a full rebuild.
